macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Blood Mixtures and Appearance as the Determining Factor

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah on mixed blood of sacrifices
Blood mixed with water: appearance as the criterion for presence
Blood mixed with wine: treating wine as water to preserve the blood
Blood mixed with animal blood or non-life blood
Rabbi Yehudah's position: blood does not nullify blood

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Sequential Mixing and the Principle of Chozer ve'Ni'ur

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's distinction: water falling into blood versus blood falling into water
The principle of chozer ve'ni'ur: does nullification reverse when proportions change?
Rav Papa on the exception of dichuy for sacrifices versus other mitzvot

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish on Multiple Forbidden Items: Issurim Mevatlim Zeh et Zeh

micro_segments.title: Case of piggul, notar, and tamei mixed together: when multiple prohibitions cancel each other
Three inferences from Reish Lakish: prohibitions nullify each other, majority rules, doubtful warning
Rashi's explanation: uncertainty in the mouth regarding which prohibition applies

macro_segments.title: Rava's Challenge: Wheat and Rice Flour and the Role of Taste

micro_segments.title: Mixed wheat and rice flour: taste determines presence despite rice being the majority
Distinction between min b'sheino mino (different kinds) and min b'mino (same kind)

macro_segments.title: The Concept of Ro'in (Treating Things As If They Were Different)

micro_segments.title: How the Mishnah uses ro'in leniently: blood in wine treated as if wine were water
Tension: how can ro'in justify leniency when min b'mino should never be batel?
Baraita on bucket with white wine or milk in mikva: majority versus Rabbi Yehudah's ro'in

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehudah and the Debate Over Ro'in as a General Principle

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Rabbi Yehudah's positions on blood, spit, and urine not nullifying each other
Abaye's resolution: ro'in belongs to his rebbe's position, majority belongs to his own
Rashi's explanation: Rabbi Yehudah resists bitul due to his rebbe's min b'mino principle
Rava's alternative: the bucket of chatat water is only tamei externally, not internally

Dichuy (pushing away) as applying only to sacrifices

Hatra'at safek (doubtful warning)
Ta'am k'ikar (taste counts like the thing itself)
Ro'in (treating as if different despite appearing same)
Piggul, notar, and tamei as three types of forbidden meat
Tevilat kelim (immersion of vessels in mikva)
Chalah (dough obligation)

Rav Papa on dichuy in mitzvot
Reish Lakish on mixed prohibitions
